mnemonics.title: UTM Inputs
mnemonics.content: U-TTM: U = Two encodings (Universal Turing machine takes: TM description + Input to simulate)

mnemonics.title: NTM Acceptance
mnemonics.content: E-4-NTM: E = Existential - NTM accepts if EXISTS at least one accepting path (not all)

mnemonics.title: Busy Beaver Definition
mnemonics.content: S-2-BB: Σ(n) = States (n) → Maximum Symbols (1s) written before halting on blank tape

mnemonics.title: Configuration Components
mnemonics.content: C-STH: Configuration = State + Tape contents + Head position

acronyms.usage: A TM that can simulate any other TM; takes two encodings as input
Has multiple transition choices; accepts if any path leads to acceptance
The fundamental model of computation with states, tape, and head
Maximum steps an n-state TM can take before halting
Maximum 1s an n-state TM can write before halting

visualTips.visualization: Imagine multiple parallel horizontal lines (tapes), each with a sliding marker (head). The machine reads all markers simultaneously and updates all lines together.
Think of a tree branching out. Each node is a configuration, each edge is a transition choice. Acceptance means at least one root-to-leaf path reaches an accepting state.
Draw consecutive boxes in a row. Each box shows the complete tape contents and head position at that step—like frames in an animation.
Picture a computer running software. The UTM is the computer (hardware), the encoded TM is the program (software), and the input string is the data being processed.

analogies.analogy: Like having multiple desks to work on different parts of a problem simultaneously, rather than doing everything on one desk and constantly reorganizing.
Like having multiple friends independently trying to solve the same puzzle. If any friend finds a solution, you succeed—without needing to try all approaches yourself.
Like a universal translator that can interpret and execute instructions written in any language—the UTM interprets any encoded TM's instructions.
Like asking: what's the most work a worker with n skills can do before deciding to stop? The busy beaver quantifies maximum computational effort.
Like a printing press that prints all books in a language one after another—it systematically lists all valid strings.
